stem: Testosterone is classified as a C19 steroid hormone. Under the stimulation of which specific pituitary hormone is it produced by the Leydig cells?
options: Follicle-stimulating hormone (FSH) acting directly on the Leydig interstitial cells
Luteinizing hormone (LH) acting directly on the Leydig interstitial cells
Adrenocorticotropic hormone (ACTH) stimulating Leydig cells via a common receptor
Prolactin acting synergistically with FSH on Leydig cell testosterone synthesis

stem: Of the total circulating testosterone in the plasma, 98% is bound to plasma proteins. Which two proteins carry this bound fraction, and in what proportions?
options: Sex hormone binding globulin (SHBG) binds 75% and albumin binds 23%
Sex hormone binding globulin (SHBG) binds 65% and albumin binds 33%
Albumin binds 65% and sex hormone binding globulin (SHBG) binds 33%
Corticosteroid binding globulin (CBG) binds 65% and albumin binds 33%

stem: The plasma testosterone level in adult males falls within a specific reference range. Which value is CORRECT for adult males?
options: 5.2 to 28.5 nmol/L in adult males
8.4 to 29.0 nmol/L in adult males
10.4 to 34.7 nmol/L in adult males
12.0 to 38.2 nmol/L in adult males

stem: The normal plasma testosterone level in adult females differs substantially from males. Which range is CORRECT for adult females?
options: 0.52 to 1.80 nmol/L
1.04 to 2.43 nmol/L
2.10 to 4.50 nmol/L
0.20 to 0.95 nmol/L

stem: Testosterone levels in the male follow a pattern of change over the lifespan. Which statement MOST accurately describes this pattern?
options: Levels rise continuously from puberty and peak at age 40 before abruptly declining
Levels remain stable throughout adult life and only decline after 70 years of age
Levels decline towards the late decade of life
Levels peak at puberty then immediately begin a steady linear decline from age 20 onwards

stem: A specific proportion of male oestrogen is produced by the aromatase activity of Sertoli cells, while the remainder comes from a different source. What percentage is produced by Sertoli cell aromatase, and what is the source of the remainder?
options: 15% from Sertoli cell aromatase; 85% from adrenal cortex aromatization of precursors
25% from Sertoli cell aromatase; 75% from extra-gonadal aromatization
40% from Sertoli cell aromatase; 60% from direct ovarian secretion in the male
50% from Sertoli cell aromatase; 50% from hepatic conversion of testosterone

stem: Testosterone is oxidized in the liver to produce 17-ketosteroids. Which THREE specific 17-ketosteroid compounds are named as the products of this hepatic oxidation?
options: Androsterone, dehydroepiandrosterone (DHEA), and androstenedione
Androsterone, epiandrosterone, and etiocholanolone
Etiocholanolone, androstenedione, and dehydroepiandrosterone
Androsterone, testosterone glucuronide, and androstenediol

stem: After hepatic oxidation to 17-ketosteroids, these compounds are conjugated with specific molecules before excretion. With which two conjugating agents are they combined, and through which routes are they excreted?
options: Conjugated with glucuronides only; excreted exclusively in urine
Conjugated with sulphates and glucuronides; excreted in urine or bile
Conjugated with sulphates only; excreted exclusively in bile
Conjugated with amino acids and sulphates; excreted in urine and sweat

stem: The mechanism of action of testosterone begins with binding to a specific receptor type. To which receptor does testosterone bind, and what is the IMMEDIATE downstream consequence of receptor binding?
options: It binds to a cell membrane G-protein coupled receptor; the receptor-hormone complex activates adenylyl cyclase to produce cAMP
It binds to an intracellular nuclear receptor; the testosterone-receptor complex then stimulates protein synthesis
It binds to a cytoplasmic receptor; the complex is transported to the nucleus where it activates RNA polymerase II
It binds to a mitochondrial receptor; the complex enhances oxidative phosphorylation driving protein anabolism

stem: Testosterone's action on the prostate, hair distribution, acne formation, and external genitalia requires conversion to a more potent androgen. What is this androgen and through which enzymatic process is it produced?
options: Androstenedione, produced by 3-beta hydroxysteroid dehydrogenase activity
Dihydrotestosterone (DHT), produced by 5-alpha reductase activity
Dehydroepiandrosterone (DHEA), produced by 17-alpha hydroxylase activity
Estradiol, produced by aromatase (cytochrome P450 19A1) activity

stem: Among the three male sex hormones produced by the testis, which is identified as the MOST important, and what additional structure also produces these androgens in small amounts?
options: DHEA is most important; the adrenal medulla also produces small amounts
Testosterone is most important; the zona reticularis of the adrenal gland also produces these androgens in small amounts
Androstenedione is most important; the zona fasciculata of the adrenal gland also produces small amounts
DHT is most important; the zona glomerulosa of the adrenal gland also produces small amounts

stem: During fetal life, testosterone exerts critical effects on sex differentiation. Which two specific actions in the fetus are attributed to testosterone?
options: It stimulates differentiation of the MÃ¼llerian duct to female internal genitalia AND promotes descent of the testis
It stimulates differentiation of the Wolffian duct to male internal genitalia AND produces MIF that aids degeneration of the female reproductive tract
It stimulates differentiation of both Wolffian and MÃ¼llerian ducts AND promotes early epiphyseal closure
It stimulates primordial germ cell differentiation AND promotes Sertoli cell proliferation in the fetus

stem: During fetal life, testosterone is also responsible for which additional structural development necessary for male anatomy?
options: Formation of the prostate gland from the urogenital sinus exclusively
Descent of the testis from the abdomen to the scrotum
Formation of the bulbourethral (Cowper's) glands from the cloacal membrane
Fusion of the labioscrotal folds to form the scrotal raphe

stem: At puberty, testosterone causes enlargement of the penis, scrotum, and other sex organs. In addition, it produces specific effects on the pubic hair distribution pattern in males. How is the male pubic hair pattern described?
options: A triangular distribution with the base facing upward toward the umbilicus
A diamond-shaped distribution with the apex extending up to the linea alba, plus baldness and receding temporal hairline
A circular distribution confined entirely to the mons pubis and perineum
A vertical distribution with hair extending only along the median perineal raphe

stem: Testosterone's effect on the pelvic bone during the secondary sexual development of the adult male produces a specific anatomical change. Which statement MOST accurately describes this effect?
options: Testosterone widens the pelvic outlet creating a broad, shallow pelvis favoring upright locomotion
Testosterone leads to broadening of shoulders, and on the pelvic bone specifically narrows the outlet forming a tunnel-like shape
Testosterone has no significant effect on the pelvic bone architecture beyond general bone mineral density enhancement
Testosterone causes bilateral acetabular enlargement creating a wider carrying angle for the lower limbs

stem: Testosterone increases basal metabolic rate by a specific percentage. What is this percentage increase?
options: By 5% above baseline
By 10% above baseline
By 15% above baseline
By 20% above baseline

stem: Testosterone increases red blood cell (RBC) production by a specific percentage range. Which range is CORRECT?
options: By 5 to 10% above baseline
By 10 to 15% above baseline
By 15 to 20% above baseline
By 20 to 25% above baseline

stem: The metabolic effects of testosterone on lipid metabolism produce a specific pattern that has clinical implications for cardiovascular disease in males. Which lipid change is CORRECT?
options: Testosterone increases HDL and decreases LDL, making men relatively protected against atherosclerosis
Testosterone increases LDL and VLDL but decreases HDL, making men more prone to atherosclerosis
Testosterone raises total cholesterol uniformly without altering the HDL to LDL ratio
Testosterone lowers total cholesterol but selectively increases VLDL making men prone to hypertriglyceridemia

stem: GnRH is a peptide hormone that initiates the hypothalamic-pituitary-testicular axis. What is the precise structural description of GnRH as stated in this curriculum?
options: A 5-amino acid peptide secreted tonically by neurons in the supraoptic nucleus
A 10-amino acid peptide secreted in pulses by neurons in the arcuate nucleus into the median eminence
A 14-amino acid peptide secreted in pulses by neurons in the paraventricular nucleus
A 10-amino acid steroid conjugate secreted continuously by hypothalamic glial cells

stem: After GnRH is secreted into the median eminence, it is transported to the anterior pituitary via a specific vascular pathway. Through which system is it transported, and which second messenger mechanism do FSH and LH use on their target cells?
options: Transported via systemic circulation; FSH/LH act via the cAMP 2nd messenger mechanism
Transported via the hypothalamohypophyseal portal system; FSH/LH act via the cAMP 2nd messenger mechanism
Transported via direct neural fibers to the anterior pituitary; FSH/LH act via IP3/calcium 2nd messenger
Transported via the posterior pituitary stalk; FSH/LH act via the phospholipids 2nd messenger exclusively

stem: FSH stimulates Sertoli cells to produce a glycoprotein that feeds back to the anterior pituitary to specifically decrease FSH production. What is this glycoprotein?
options: Activin â a glycoprotein that selectively inhibits pituitary FSH secretion
Inhibin â a glycoprotein that selectively feeds back on the anterior pituitary to decrease FSH
Androgen binding protein â which transports testosterone to the pituitary to suppress FSH
Sex hormone binding globulin (SHBG) â which reduces free testosterone available for FSH suppression

stem: The Sertoli cells produce Sex Hormone Binding Globulin (SHBG). Testosterone also has an important local action on the Sertoli cells. What is this local action?
options: Testosterone stimulates Sertoli cells to produce MIF and suppress Leydig cell function via paracrine inhibition
Testosterone acts locally on the Sertoli cells to aid spermatogenesis
Testosterone stimulates Sertoli cells to increase inhibin production for FSH suppression
Testosterone locally stimulates Sertoli cells to release mature spermatozoa into the tubular lumen

stem: The semen consists of spermatozoa and secretions from multiple glands in specific proportions. Which combination of percentages is CORRECT for spermatozoa, seminal vesicle secretions, and prostatic secretions respectively?
options: Spermatozoa 5%, seminal vesicle 65%, prostate 30%
Spermatozoa 10%, seminal vesicle 60%, prostate 30%
Spermatozoa 15%, seminal vesicle 55%, prostate 30%
Spermatozoa 10%, seminal vesicle 55%, prostate 35%

stem: Secretions from the bulbourethral (Cowper's) glands and urethral glands constitute a specific small proportion of the total semen volume. What proportion do they constitute?
options: Less than 2%
Less than 7%
Approximately 10%
Approximately 15%

stem: Immediately after ejaculation, semen is watery and then quickly congeals. After what precise time interval does the semen then undergo SECONDARY liquefaction?
options: After 10 minutes it undergoes secondary liquefaction
After 20 minutes it undergoes secondary liquefaction
After 30 minutes it undergoes secondary liquefaction
After 60 minutes it undergoes secondary liquefaction

stem: Spermatozoa obtain their nutrition through a specific metabolic pathway. Which pathway and specific substrate are used?
options: Aerobic metabolism of glucose to ATP via the Krebs cycle
Anaerobic metabolism of fructose to pyruvate
Aerobic metabolism of glutamine via the pentose phosphate pathway
Anaerobic glycolysis of glucose to lactate for energy production

stem: The WHO 2010 lower reference value for semen volume differs from the 1999 reference. What is the WHO 2010 lower reference range for semen volume?
options: 1.0 to 5.5 ml
1.5 to 6.0 ml
1.6 to 6.8 ml
2.0 to 6.0 ml

stem: According to the WHO 2010 lower reference values, the minimum semen concentration for normal fertility is stated. What is this minimum concentration?
options: At least 10 x 10â¶ cells/ml
At least 15 x 10â¶ cells/ml
At least 20 x 10â¶ cells/ml
At least 25 x 10â¶ cells/ml

stem: According to the WHO 2010 lower reference values, what is the minimum percentage of progressively motile spermatozoa required for normal semen?
options: More than 25% progressively motile
More than 32% progressively motile
More than 40% progressively motile
More than 50% progressively motile

stem: The WHO 2010 lower reference for sperm morphology differs from the 1999 standard. What percentage of normal sperm forms is required according to WHO 2010?
options: More than 2% normal forms
More than 4% normal forms
More than 7% normal forms
More than 14% normal forms

stem: The WHO 2010 reference for white blood cell count in semen specifies an upper limit. What is this upper limit?
options: Less than 0.5 x 10â¶ WBC/ml
Less than 1 x 10â¶ WBC/ml
Less than 2 x 10â¶ WBC/ml
Less than 5 x 10â¶ WBC/ml

stem: A key comparison between WHO 1999 and WHO 2010 semen parameters involves the minimum sperm concentration threshold. How did this threshold change between 1999 and 2010?
options: Decreased from 25 million/ml (1999) to 20 million/ml (2010)
Decreased from 20 million/ml (1999) to 15 million/ml (2010)
Decreased from 15 million/ml (1999) to 10 million/ml (2010)
Increased from 15 million/ml (1999) to 20 million/ml (2010)

stem: The semen analysis requires a standardized pre-collection abstinence period and a specific time constraint for examination after collection. What are these two requirements?
options: 3 days abstinence; examination within 1 hour of collection
5 days abstinence; examination within 2 hours of collection
7 days abstinence; examination within 4 hours of collection
2 days abstinence; examination within 30 minutes of collection

stem: The clinical term used when an ejaculate contains ZERO spermatozoa is:
options: Aspermia â the complete absence of any ejaculate
Oligospermia â inadequate sperm count below 15,000,000/ml
Azoospermia â ejaculate containing no spermatozoa
Necrospermia â ejaculate containing only dead spermatozoa

stem: Oligospermia is defined by a specific numerical threshold. Which sperm count constitutes oligospermia?
options: Less than 5,000,000 spermatozoa per ml
Less than 10,000,000 spermatozoa per ml
Less than 15,000,000 spermatozoa per ml
Less than 20,000,000 spermatozoa per ml

stem: Aspermia is a condition that is distinct from azoospermia. What precisely does aspermia describe?
options: An ejaculate that contains no living spermatozoa (all spermatozoa are dead)
A situation in which there is no ejaculate at all
An ejaculate that contains fewer than 5 million spermatozoa per millilitre
A condition of retrograde ejaculation where semen enters the bladder

stem: Normal semen has specific physical properties. Which combination correctly states the specific gravity and reaction (pH) of normal semen?
options: Specific gravity of 1.015; reaction acidic (pH less than 7.0)
Specific gravity of 1.028; reaction alkaline (pH greater than 7.2)
Specific gravity of 1.035; reaction neutral (pH 7.0 to 7.2)
Specific gravity of 1.020; reaction alkaline (pH greater than 7.4)

stem: Secretions from the seminal vesicle have a specific physical appearance and are rich in particular substances. Which description is MOST complete?
options: Thin, colourless, and acidic; rich in zinc, citrate, and acid phosphatase
Viscid, alkaline, and yellow; rich in vitamin C, fructose, and high K+
Viscid, acidic, and white; rich in fructose, phospholipids, and spermine
Clear, alkaline, and watery; rich in prostaglandins and cholesterol

stem: Beyond vitamin C, fructose, and high potassium, the seminal vesicle secretion contains additional specific substances. Which combination is listed in this curriculum?
options: Sodium, calcium, zinc, and acid phosphatase
Phosphoryl choline, fibrinogen, citric acid, pepsinogen, and prostaglandin
Spermine, fibrinolysin, and cholesterol
Hyaluronidase, HCO3-, and spermidine

stem: The seminal vesicle secretion confers a specific physical property to the semen. What property does it provide?
options: The acidic buffering capacity that protects spermatozoa from the alkaline vaginal environment
Mucus consistency to the semen
The principal energy substrate (fructose) that fuels sperm motility after deposition
The milky appearance that is characteristic of normal freshly ejaculated semen

stem: Prostatic secretions have a distinctive physical appearance and a unique property when elevated in the bloodstream. Which statement MOST accurately describes prostatic secretions?
options: They are viscid, yellow, and rich in fructose; fibrinogen in the secretion contributes to initial semen coagulation
They are thin, colourless, and confer a peculiar odour; acid phosphatase appears in abnormally high levels in blood with malignant conditions of the prostate
They are thin, colourless, and rich in zinc; alkaline phosphatase is the clinical marker elevated in prostatic malignancy
They are viscid, alkaline, and contain primarily prostaglandins and vitamin C

stem: The prostatic secretion contains a comprehensive list of specific biochemical components. Which combination is CORRECTLY and MOST completely stated?
options: Na, Ca, Zn, citrate, cholesterol, phospholipids, acid phosphatase, spermine, fibrinolysin, clotting enzymes, proteolytic enzymes
K, Mg, Zn, fructose, fibrinogen, vitamin C, pepsinogen, prostaglandin, citric acid
Na, K, HCO3-, spermine, acid phosphatase, and phosphoryl choline only
Ca, Zn, spermine, and alkaline phosphatase exclusively

stem: Prostatic secretions confer a specific visual property to the overall semen. What appearance does the prostatic secretion give to semen?
options: A yellow viscous appearance from the prostaglandin content
A milky appearance to the semen
A watery clear appearance that dilutes the viscous seminal vesicle contribution
A faint greenish tinge due to the zinc and cholesterol content

stem: A specific part of the spermatozoon is responsible for its penetrating power, a different part stores energy abundantly, and a third part maintains the propelling ability. Which assignment is CORRECT?
options: Head: energy store; middle piece: penetrating power; tail: propulsion
Head: penetrating power; middle piece: abundant energy store; tail: propelling ability
Head: propelling ability; middle piece: penetrating enzymes; tail: energy store
Head: acrosomal enzymes for penetration; tail: energy store from mitochondria; middle piece: propulsion

stem: During sperm maturation and transport, two critical processes occur. What are these two processes named in this curriculum?
options: Liquefaction and capacitation
Capacitation and acrosomal reaction
Acrosomal reaction and zona pellucida dissolution
Capacitation and corona radiata dispersal

stem: Capacitation is not fully understood. What is currently believed to be the mechanism by which capacitation occurs in the female genital tract?
options: The acidic vaginal environment activates acrosomal hyaluronidase by lowering pH around the spermatozoon
Uterine and fallopian tube fluids wash away the various inhibitory factors that can suppress sperm activity
Progesterone secreted by cumulus cells directly triggers the acrosomal membrane to fuse with the plasma membrane
Capacitation is caused by the hyperosmotic cervical mucus that triggers calcium influx into the spermatozoon

stem: The acrosomal reaction involves the release of specific enzymes necessary for penetration of the ovum's protective layers. Which enzyme is specifically named, and which two structures must be penetrated?
options: Neuraminidase and proteolytic enzymes; penetrate the zona reaction and plasma membrane
Hyaluronidase and proteolytic enzymes; penetrate the corona radiata and zona pellucida
Acrosin and phospholipase; penetrate the cumulus oophorus and plasma membrane
Hyaluronidase and collagenase; penetrate the zona pellucida and oolemma

stem: Sperm is stored in the epididymis and vas deferens for at least a specific period. During storage, sperm acquire one characteristic but lack another. What does sperm acquire and what does it still lack during storage?
options: Sperm acquires morphological maturity but lacks flagellar formation
Sperm acquires motility characteristics but has no full potential for fertilization
Sperm acquires full fertilization potential but lacks progressive motility
Sperm acquires nuclear condensation but lacks the acrosome needed for penetration

stem: Once deposited in the female genital tract and after developing full motility, sperm swim up at a specific speed and reach the fallopian tubes within a defined time. What is the swimming speed and the time to reach the tubes?
options: 1 mm/min; reaching tubes within 15 to 30 minutes
3 mm/min; reaching tubes within 30 to 60 minutes
5 mm/min; reaching tubes within 10 to 20 minutes
2 mm/min; reaching tubes within 60 to 90 minutes

stem: Three specific mechanisms aid the movement of sperm through the epididymis during transport. Which combination is CORRECT?
options: Sperm flagellar activity, uterine contractile waves, and ciliary action of the cervical epithelium
Pressure of Sertoli fluids, peristaltic movements, and ciliary action of ductular cells
Testosterone-driven vas deferens contractions, Sertoli fluid pressure, and periductal myoid cells
Gravity-assisted descent, ciliary activity, and osmotic pressure from the rete testis fluid

stem: Castration specifically performed as part of cancer treatment is done to treat which malignancy, and what is the clinical objective of this intervention?
options: Done as part of treatment for testicular cancer to prevent metastatic spread via lymphatics
Done as part of treatment for cancer of the prostate to reduce prostatic stimulation
Done as part of treatment for bladder cancer to eliminate androgen-driven urothelial proliferation
Done as part of treatment for adrenal cancer to remove the source of excess androgen production

stem: Castration performed before puberty results in a characteristic body habitus. What is the specific term used to describe the tall, eunuchoid body proportions resulting from late epiphyseal closure after pre-pubertal castration?
options: Klinefelter habitus
Eunuchoid habitus
Marfanoid habitus
Acromegaloid habitus

stem: Precocious puberty in males is defined as secondary sexual characteristics occurring before a specific age. What is this age?
options: Before 7 years of age
Before 8 years of age
Before 9 years of age
Before 10 years of age

stem: Delayed puberty in males is defined by failure of testicular development at a specific age. At what age is testicular development EXPECTED to have occurred, and failure at this age constitutes delayed puberty?
options: Failure to occur at 16 years of age
Failure to occur at 18 years of age
Failure to occur at 20 years of age
Failure to occur at 22 years of age

stem: Male hypogonadism occurring when testes of a male fetus are non-functional during fetal life is classified into two distinct subtypes. What determines whether it is hypergonadotropic or hypogonadotropic?
options: It is hypergonadotropic when due to hypothalamic or pituitary failure, and hypogonadotropic when due to testicular failure
It is hypergonadotropic when due to testicular failure, and hypogonadotropic when due to hypothalamic or pituitary failure
Both types have identical gonadotropin patterns; they are distinguished only by testosterone levels
It is hypergonadotropic when LH alone is elevated, and hypogonadotropic when only FSH is elevated

stem: Testicular hypergonadism occurs when testicular tumors produce excessive androgens. Which combination of effects results from this excessive androgen production?
options: Excessive muscular and hair growth, and potential precocious puberty if it occurs early
Excessive bone density with early epiphyseal fusion, and suppression of gonadotropins causing infertility
Gynaecomastia, testicular atrophy from FSH suppression, and azoospermia
Short stature from early epiphyseal closure, polycythaemia, and aggressive cardiovascular disease

stem: CASE STUDY: A 38-year-old man presents to a fertility clinic. Semen analysis shows: volume 1.8 ml, concentration 13 x 10â¶ cells/ml, progressive motility 28%, morphology 3% normal forms. Based on WHO 2010 lower reference values, which parameter FIRST defines this semen sample as abnormal?
options: Morphology at 3% â below the WHO 2010 threshold of more than 4% normal forms
Concentration at 13 x 10â¶ cells/ml â below the WHO 2010 minimum of 15 x 10â¶ cells/ml
Progressive motility at 28% â below the WHO 2010 minimum of 32% progressively motile
Volume at 1.8 ml â below the WHO 2010 minimum lower reference of 1.6 ml but technically within the 1.6-6.8 ml range

stem: CASE STUDY: For the same 38-year-old man (Q57), the concentration of 13 x 10â¶ cells/ml means he meets the clinical definition of which sperm parameter abnormality?
options: Azoospermia â as his count falls below 15 million/ml making the ejaculate functionally equivalent to no sperm
Oligospermia â as his concentration is below the threshold of 15,000,000 per ml
Aspermia â as his low count means the sample has insufficient volume to contain adequate sperm numbers
Necrospermia â as his low motility combined with low count suggests primarily dead spermatozoa

stem: CASE STUDY: A 42-year-old man is referred for semen analysis after a year of infertility. The laboratory reports no ejaculate was produced despite orgasm. The nurse correctly documents this as which condition?
options: Azoospermia â complete absence of spermatozoa from the ejaculate
Aspermia â complete absence of any ejaculate
Retrograde ejaculation â ejaculate was present but diverted into the bladder during orgasm
Oligospermia â inadequate sperm count due to failure of ejaculation mechanism

stem: CASE STUDY: A clinical biochemist explains why the acid phosphatase in prostatic secretion is clinically significant. A 65-year-old man presents with abnormally high blood levels of acid phosphatase. What is the MOST likely implication of this finding based on the curriculum?
options: It indicates Sertoli cell dysfunction with impaired inhibin production causing compensatory FSH elevation
It suggests a malignant condition of the prostate, as acid phosphatase appears in abnormally high blood levels in prostatic malignancy
It indicates excessive androgenic stimulation causing hypertrophy of the prostate without malignancy
It indicates primary testicular failure causing compensatory adrenal androgen production

stem: CASE STUDY: A 29-year-old male patient with known orchitis following a mumps infection presents with infertility. The nurse explains the link between orchitis and infertility. Which mechanism MOST directly explains how orchitis impairs fertility?
options: Orchitis causes autoimmune destruction of Leydig cells, eliminating testosterone production needed for secondary sexual characteristics
Orchitis (testicular inflammation) reduces spermatogenesis by damaging the seminiferous epithelium
Orchitis disrupts the hypothalamic-pituitary-testicular axis by releasing inflammatory cytokines that inhibit GnRH pulses
Orchitis causes fibrous obliteration of the vas deferens preventing transport of otherwise normal spermatozoa

stem: CASE STUDY: A 55-year-old man with known prostate cancer is offered castration (orchidectomy) as part of his management. His wife asks the nurse how removing the testes will help treat prostate cancer. The nurse correctly explains that:
options: The testes produce FSH which directly stimulates prostate cancer cell proliferation; orchidectomy removes this stimulatory hormone
The testes produce testosterone under LH stimulation; removing the testes eliminates testosterone, thus reducing prostatic stimulation that drives cancer growth
The testes produce oestrogen that paradoxically promotes prostate cancer; removing this oestrogen source causes cancer regression
The testes produce DHT that stimulates Wolffian duct derivatives including the prostate; orchidectomy eliminates this growth signal

stem: CASE STUDY: A couple presents with infertility. The male partner's semen analysis is submitted. Prior to collection, the laboratory recommends a specific preparation period. Which recommendation is standard per the curriculum?
options: 3 days abstinence with collection by masturbation and examination within 1 hour
5 days abstinence with collection by masturbation and examination within 2 hours
7 days abstinence with collection by masturbation and examination within 4 hours
2 days abstinence with collection during intercourse using a special condom

stem: CASE STUDY: A nurse educator explains testosterone's effect on erythropoiesis to a group of nursing students. A student asks why men have higher haemoglobin levels than women. The nurse correctly answers that testosterone increases RBC production by which specific percentage above baseline?

CASE STUDY: A 32-year-old man is found to have bilateral undescended testes (cryptorchidism) that were never corrected. He now has azoospermia but normal testosterone levels and normal secondary sexual characteristics. The nurse explains why his testosterone is normal despite azoospermia. Which explanation is MOST correct?
options: Leydig cells do not require optimal scrotal temperature for testosterone synthesis; only spermatogenesis is temperature-sensitive, so testosterone production is preserved despite cryptorchidism
Adrenal androgens fully compensate for the absent testicular testosterone in cryptorchidism
The elevated intra-abdominal temperature in cryptorchidism selectively damages Sertoli cells but not Leydig cells
Leydig cells are stimulated by FSH rather than LH, and FSH pulses are temperature-independent

stem: CASE STUDY: A 12-year-old boy presents with advanced secondary sexual characteristics including deep voice, adult pubic hair pattern, and penile enlargement. Investigation reveals markedly elevated testosterone from a testicular Leydig cell tumor. This presentation is MOST consistent with which abnormality of the male reproductive system?
options: Hypogonadotropic hypogonadism causing paradoxical activation of testosterone receptors
Hypergonadism from a testicular tumor producing excessive androgens, leading to precocious puberty
Precocious puberty from gonadotropin-dependent central activation of the HPO axis
Constitutional precocious puberty with incidental Leydig cell hyperplasia

stem: CASE STUDY: A 24-year-old male athlete is being counselled about the risks of anabolic steroid use. The nurse explains the cardiovascular risk using testosterone's metabolic effects. Which statement MOST accurately explains this risk?
options: Anabolic steroids increase HDL and reduce LDL, paradoxically worsening endothelial function through reverse cholesterol transport
Anabolic steroids (androgens) increase LDL and VLDL while decreasing HDL, making users more prone to atherosclerosis
Anabolic steroids increase total cholesterol uniformly without altering the HDL/LDL ratio, only modestly increasing cardiovascular risk
Anabolic steroids primarily cause hypertension through sodium and water retention without significant lipid profile changes

stem: CASE STUDY: A researcher studying sperm capacitation in vitro notes that sperm placed directly in culture medium without uterine fluid fail to fertilize the ova. After adding fallopian tube fluid, fertilization succeeds. What is the MOST likely explanation based on the curriculum?
options: Fallopian tube fluid provides the fructose substrate that activates anaerobic metabolism in capacitated sperm
Uterine and fallopian tube fluids wash away inhibitory factors that suppress sperm activity, enabling capacitation
Fallopian tube fluid activates the acrosomal hyaluronidase by providing calcium ions for the acrosomal reaction
Fallopian tube fluid contains progesterone that directly triggers zona pellucida receptor expression on the sperm head

stem: CASE STUDY: A pathologist examining a semen sample notes fibrinogen and fibrinolysin in the report. A student asks which gland is responsible for these components and their physiological role. Which answer is MOST correct?
options: Fibrinogen comes from the seminal vesicle causing initial coagulation; fibrinolysin from the prostate causes subsequent liquefaction
Both fibrinogen and fibrinolysin come from the prostate; fibrinolysin degrades the fibrinogen clot formed initially
Fibrinogen and fibrinolysin both originate from the seminal vesicle; they maintain viscosity throughout storage
Fibrinogen comes from bulbourethral glands; fibrinolysin comes from urethral glands

stem: CASE STUDY: A 17-year-old male with Klinefelter's syndrome (47XXY) is evaluated for delayed testicular development at age 22. He has small testes, gynaecomastia, and very high FSH/LH with very low testosterone. The nurse correctly classifies this as which type of hypogonadism and explains the gonadotropin pattern?
options: Hypogonadotropic hypogonadism with low FSH and LH because the pituitary has failed to respond to the absent testosterone
Hypergonadotropic hypogonadism with high FSH and LH because the testes have failed as primary organs, removing negative feedback
Eugonadotrophic hypogonadism with normal FSH and LH despite end-organ testicular resistance
Hypogonadotropic hypogonadism with selective LH deficiency preserving FSH for residual spermatogenesis

stem: Testosterone is a ___ steroid produced by the ___ cells under ___ stimulation.
Of plasma testosterone, 98% is bound to proteins: sex hormone binding globulin binds ___% while ___% binds to albumin; only ___% is free.
The plasma testosterone level is ___ to ___ nmol/L in adult males and ___ to ___ nmol/L in adult females.
Testosterone is oxidized in the liver to produce 17-keto steroids: ___, epiandrosterone, and ___.
After hepatic oxidation, 17-keto steroids are conjugated with ___ and ___ before being excreted in urine or bile.
Testosterone's action on the prostate, hair distribution, acne formation, and external genitalia is mediated via conversion to ___, which is a product of ___ reductase activity.
During fetal life, testosterone stimulates differentiation of the ___ duct to male internal genitalia and produces ___ that aids degeneration of the female reproductive tract.
Testosterone increases the basal metabolic rate by ___%, increases RBC production by ___ to ___%, and increases LDL and VLDL while decreasing ___ making men more prone to atherosclerosis.
GnRH is a ___-amino acid peptide secreted in ___ by neurons in the arcuate nucleus into the median eminence, and is transported to the anterior pituitary via the ___ portal system.
FSH stimulates Sertoli cells to produce the glycoprotein ___ which feeds back on the ___ pituitary to decrease FSH production; FSH and LH act on target cells via the ___ 2nd messenger mechanism.
The aromatase activity of Sertoli cells produces about ___% of oestrogens in males, while the rest of male oestrogen is produced by ___ gonadal aromatization.
Semen consists of spermatozoa (___%), secretions from the seminal vesicle (___%), secretion from the prostate (___%), and secretions from bulbourethral and urethral glands (less than 7%).
The WHO 2010 lower reference values for semen include: volume ___ to ___ ml, concentration at least ___ x 10â¶ cells/ml, and progressive motility more than ___%.

When the ejaculate contains no spermatozoa, the term is ___; when there is an inadequate number of spermatozoa (less than 15,000,000/ml), the term is ___; and when there is no ejaculate at all, the term is ___.
The liquefaction time for normal semen should be within ___ minutes, the morphology threshold requires more than ___% normal forms (WHO 2010), and the WBC count must be less than ___ x 10â¶/ml.
Seminal vesicle secretions are viscid, ___, and yellow; they are rich in vitamin C, ___, and high K+; and they also contain phosphoryl choline, fibrinogen, citric acid, ___, and prostaglandin.
Prostatic secretions are thin, colourless, and confer a ___ odour; they give semen its ___ appearance; and acid ___ appears in abnormally high blood levels in malignant conditions of the prostate.

Normal semen has a specific gravity of ___ and a ___ reaction (pH greater than 7.2). The seminal vesicle secretion provides ___ consistency to semen while the prostate secretion provides the ___ appearance.
acceptableAnswers: 1.028 / alkaline reaction / mucus consistency / milky appearance

stem: In the spermatozoon, the ___ has excellent penetrating power, the ___ has abundant energy store, and the ___ maintains propelling ability.

During sperm maturation, two processes occur: ___ (where uterine and fallopian fluids wash away inhibitory factors) and the ___ reaction (where hyaluronidase and proteolytic enzymes are released for penetration).

Sperm is stored in the ___ and vas for at least a ___, during which it acquires ___ characteristics but no full potential for fertilization; deposited in the female genital tract, sperm swim up at ___ mm/min reaching the tubes within ___ to ___ minutes.
acceptableAnswers: epididymis / one month / motility characteristics / 3mm/min / 30-60 minutes

stem: Sperm movement through the epididymis is aided by pressure of ___ fluids, ___ movements, and ciliary actions of ductular cells.
Castration is also called ___ and when done before puberty, individuals are usually ___ than normal due to ___ epiphyseal closure, producing a body habitus called eunuchoid.
acceptableAnswers: orchidectomy / taller than normal / late (delayed) epiphyseal closure

stem: Precocious puberty in males occurs when secondary sexual characteristics appear before the normal age of ___ years; delayed puberty occurs when testicular development fails to occur at ___ years.
